If we look back at the case of the 2008 subprime mortgage crisis, it actually brewed and erupted about seven years after China joined the World Trade Organization (WTO). At that time, China's accession led to the loss of about 35% of manufacturing jobs in the United States. These jobless workers fell into poverty, forced to take out subprime loans, and were unable to repay their debts. Although the default rate only rose slightly to single digits, the leverage effect ultimately triggered a financial crisis. The pace of AI advancement is far faster than the rate at which assembly line workers were laid off. We've seen companies like The Block lay off 40% of their staff overnight. In the US, many companies, based on flexible labor agreements such as "casual hiring," are discussing similar layoff plans. Once these companies discover that AI can perform tasks more efficiently, they could quickly lay off 10%, 20%, or even 30% of their staff. My point is not that all white-collar jobs will disappear, but that just 10% to 20% of white-collar jobs being replaced would be enough to trigger a leverage effect in the banking system, leading to a chain reaction. This situation is similar to a "Minsky Moment": when the market suddenly realizes that certain assets are worthless, it triggers a panic sell-off. While the full impact may take two to three years to materialize, the market reaction could be almost immediate. We cannot accurately predict when this will happen, but once a certain "collective consensus" forms in the market, such as "AI disruption has arrived, and a large number of white-collar workers are being laid off," people may begin to question the value of financial stocks. At that point, bank stocks could plummet by 60% to 70% in just a few days, depositors will shift their savings from small and medium-sized banks to large banks like JP Morgan and Citibank, and the Federal Reserve will have to intervene quickly. Therefore, although the full impact may take two to three years to materialize, the market's perception of this change could be very rapid. The formation of a certain "collective consensus," such as "AI disruption has already had a significant impact on the economy," could trigger a massive market reaction.

The Impact of Social Unrest and Economic Pressure on the Crypto Market

Natalie Brunell: Are you worried about the potential social impact of these events?

Social division in the United States is becoming increasingly severe, and we are seeing widespread public discontent and escalating social unrest. Politically, people are becoming increasingly tribalized and polarized. And as more people lose their jobs, some seem to be opting for candidates who promise "free benefits." This core sense of frustration seems to have a ripple effect, and many people don't realize that they need to own and accumulate hard assets like Bitcoin.

Arthur Hayes:

Indeed, the social contract between labor and capital differs across countries. In the United States, this contract appears very fragile because capital clearly dominates, while the rights of workers are relatively neglected. Elsewhere, the situation is more balanced. Therefore, I do believe that we will face a highly divisive era in the future.

Many people once considered themselves wealthy, but when they lose their jobs, they find themselves among those they once looked down upon, dependent on government assistance. How will this affect their self-esteem? How will they express their discontent? Through political means, or more direct methods, such as taking to the streets with weapons? Some might oppose the construction of data centers, or target tech giants like Elon Musk, Sam Altman, and Mark Zuckerberg, arguing that they are robbing ordinary people of their American Dream by destroying jobs for excessive profits. I cannot predict how all this will ultimately unfold, but one thing is certain: future social evolution will not be linear, but rather fraught with uncertainty and fluctuations. This will inevitably be a period of division, as America's existing social contract needs to be redefined. As for how this contract will change, I cannot give a definitive answer, but it will undoubtedly be a challenging and painful process. Can Bitcoin solve the global affordability problem? Natalie Brunell: Many people are very frustrated with the current situation because they feel they can't afford a house and can't participate in the capital and wealth accumulation of the past few decades. I think Bitcoin seems like an obvious solution because people can accumulate it little by little. However, there are still many people who are resistant to Bitcoin. I'm very surprised that there are still so many people against Bitcoin despite the great progress we've made. Arthur Hayes: I don't think buying a little Bitcoin will really solve your problem. If you earn $250,000 a year but suddenly lose your job and can't pay your $750,000 mortgage and $50,000 monthly credit card bills, then Bitcoin won't help you. Of course, for those who accumulate Bitcoin in the long term, it is indeed a good asset, and I think the price of Bitcoin will continue to rise in the long run. But for those who once thought they were rich but now find themselves in trouble, Bitcoin won't immediately solve their financial problems. They may realize that they are spending too much money each month to maintain their so-called "ideal lifestyle." I think Bitcoin is more suitable for those with spare capital and who recognize it as a potential solution. If you belong to a group that might be replaced by AI or economic changes, then you need to rethink your lifestyle and spending patterns. For example, reassess whether you really need those expensive consumer electronics or whether you need to live in a high-cost city or community. As the saying goes, "the first to sell sells best." When people start to realize they need to cut back on spending, who will take over those expensive properties? If your income drops from $250,000 to $75,000, you may find yourself having to make significant lifestyle adjustments. Natalie Brunell: Even so, among all the possible solutions or "life rafts," don't you think people have very limited choices? If someone is living paycheck to paycheck, their job is no longer stable, they have no assets, and they even face the risk of being laid off, with a very uncertain future, what else can help them in this situation? Arthur Hayes: To be honest, I've never experienced anything like this myself, so I can only try to analyze it from an external perspective. But I think the most important thing is to examine your cost of living and reduce unnecessary expenses as much as possible. For example, consider what things you really don't need. You might have been used to shopping casually on Amazon in the past, but now you might need to switch to more economical options. The key is to quickly adjust your finances so that when a crisis really comes, you are prepared for it and better able to find new ways out. What are Bitcoin investors' expectations for the market? Natalie Brunell: I've met some investors who only started getting involved with Bitcoin at the peak of the last bull market. They entered the market when Bitcoin prices reached $50,000 or even $60,000. Now they are very frustrated because of the huge price fluctuations, even crashes, coupled with the overall collapse of the crypto market. They feel that their investment has not brought any life-changing returns. These people often say, "Nothing has changed for me." Sometimes I wonder what I can say to them to get them back on track and continue accumulating Bitcoin slowly through dollar-cost averaging. After all, this investment hasn't brought them any substantial change in wealth. They might feel that all the opportunities are over, and those who entered the market early, such as those who bought Bitcoin or participated in mining before 2017, were able to accumulate large amounts of Bitcoin through ordinary income. Now, those opportunities seem to have disappeared. They might wonder, what can they accumulate now? Arthur Hayes: I think the first thing is to adjust your expectations. Whether you buy Bitcoin, stocks, or real estate, the market's essence isn't to make you money; its essence is to take your money away. If your investment period is too short and your expectations are too high, then you'll take risks, even using leverage, because you're eager to profit quickly. You might think, “I need to leverage it to make money faster because it’s a transformative asset.” But have you ever considered how those people you see on TikTok who have made a fortune with Bitcoin survived the early days? How did they feel when Bitcoin plummeted from $250 to $70 in 2013? How did they persevere when it dropped from $1300 to $135 in 2014? Many people only see how much money these people have made today, without realizing that Bitcoin’s volatility in its early days was three to four times higher than it is now. If you bought $10,000 worth of Bitcoin at $100 in the early days, you might sell it when the price rose to $200, or sell it out of disappointment when the price dropped to $99, because it didn’t meet your expectations for short-term price performance. So, what I’m trying to say is that Bitcoin won’t save your finances immediately; its value lies more in long-term accumulation. As the saying goes, "Stocks are the best choice for long-term investment," and this applies equally to Bitcoin. Compound interest and time are the most powerful forces in the process of capital accumulation, but the key is time. If you can persist and invest consistently over the long term, Bitcoin can help you accumulate wealth exponentially. However, it's unrealistic to expect the market or an asset to bring "life-changing" returns in just six months. Of course, you might see some people become rich overnight due to good luck, but I bet they'll lose all their earnings within the next six months because they'll continue to believe they can earn the same wealth through risky trading strategies. Bitcoin in China: Current Status and Future Natalie Brunell: What is the adoption rate and investment mentality of Bitcoin in your region, or in places like China? Because I've heard different opinions. Arthur Hayes: Actually, the Chinese government and some local governments are still engaged in Bitcoin mining. If you look at the IP data of the Bitcoin network, you'll find that China's contribution to global hashrate still accounts for 20% to 30%. As for why China shut down most non-governmental mining farms, the main reason is the adjustment of its energy policy, especially given the current war between the US, Israel, and Iran. China does not want to rely on imported oil or other hydrocarbon energy sources. The government wants to use energy resources for things that are more in line with the country's long-term development goals, such as producing electric vehicles (EVs), batteries, and humanoid robots, rather than for mining Bitcoin. This is one of the important reasons why the Chinese Communist Party is pushing for the withdrawal of Bitcoin mining, because they believe that mining consumes a lot of energy but does not directly help the country's economic goals. However, despite this, there are still many idle energy resources within China, allowing some smaller local governments to continue Bitcoin mining "quietly" under the supervision of the central government. Therefore, many large mining farms are still operating in China, and these farms are usually linked to local or central governments. So, in general, while China nominally bans Bitcoin mining, the reality is not entirely so. How do governments view Bitcoin? Natalie Brunell: I suspect the Chinese government may have accumulated a considerable amount of Bitcoin at the national level, which may be one reason why the US is reluctant to disclose how much Bitcoin we actually hold. Perhaps it's related to our adversarial relationship with China. What impact would it have on the situation if China held more Bitcoin than the US? Arthur Hayes: Maybe, but I'm not sure. However, I don't think focusing on how much Bitcoin a sovereign nation holds is very meaningful. Even if we assume China holds 200,000 Bitcoins, the value of which might be billions of dollars, is still small compared to the gold market. The gold market itself is still a small market compared to the dollar stock market or the real estate market. Therefore, I don't think either the US or Chinese government would lose sleep over how much Bitcoin they hold. In terms of the overall scale of national wealth, Bitcoin's influence is far from sufficient. Natalie Brunell: So, do you disagree with Max Kaiser's view? He believes that a so-called "hash war" might break out in the future, where governments compete for Bitcoin mining power and try to accumulate as much Bitcoin as possible. Arthur Hayes: I don't agree with this view. I don't think sovereign nations care that much about Bitcoin. Even in the US, Bitcoin is more often used as a tool to attract voters, as you can see from some of the policies of the Trump administration. They might promise to fight for certain voter groups during the campaign, but the policies ultimately implemented often don't entirely match the promises. Whether you agree or not, this shows that Bitcoin in the US plays more of a political tool to win votes. This is even less likely to happen in China. The Chinese government isn't concerned with Bitcoin itself; they're more interested in the internationalization of the RMB and how to use it for international trade. What people do with Bitcoin is irrelevant to them. The Current State and Challenges of Bitcoin's Popularity Natalie Brunell: Bitcoin is a revolutionary technology that frees us from the constraints of traditional financial systems and fiat currencies. What do you think the future holds for Bitcoin in the next 5 to 10 years? From the perspective of popularity and growth, how do you foresee its future development? Of course, rising prices certainly help drive its adoption, right? Arthur Hayes: I think rising prices are indeed a key factor driving Bitcoin's popularity. Rising prices themselves are like a viral mechanism for Bitcoin's spread. Why do people even hear about Bitcoin? Because its price surged in 2013, the media started reporting on it, I saw articles about it, and then I became interested in the technology and began to learn more about it. So, if the price of Bitcoin rises from its current $66,000 to $500,000 within the next five years, it will inevitably attract a large number of new users, while also drawing renewed attention to Bitcoin's technology and potential. This price leap is a key driver of Bitcoin's widespread adoption. So, what is the fundamental driving force behind all this? The answer is liquidity. That is, how much fiat currency is being created in the market, whether it's US dollars, euros, yen, or yuan. As long as these currencies continue to be printed in large quantities (and I don't think this trend will stop), mathematically speaking, as the money supply expands, the price of Bitcoin will rise accordingly. Of course, price volatility and path uncertainty are unavoidable. For example, due to war or other unforeseen events, the price of Bitcoin could fall to $20,000 in the coming weeks. However, in the long run, the existing fractional-reserve banking system forces governments to continuously print money, a trend that is almost irreversible. It is this continuous increase in money supply that will drive the long-term rise in Bitcoin prices. Ultimately, the price increase will attract more people to the market, and this attraction is the core driving force behind Bitcoin's widespread adoption. Will retail investors return to the Bitcoin market? Natalie Brunell: Do you think we will see another cycle of renewed enthusiasm from retail investors? The dominant force in the market seems to have shifted to institutional investors. Do you think there will be another surge led by ordinary people in the future? Arthur Hayes: Of course, but perhaps this time the protagonist won't be Bitcoin, but some other cryptocurrency. It could be something more closely related to cultural products, such as NFTs or even something like memes. While I know many people lost a lot of money on Meme in the last cycle, and the craze seems to have passed, if you want to know what the main driving force of the last craze was, it was Meme trading. New hot topics will definitely emerge in the future. Because I believe that central banks around the world will inevitably continue to print money to ensure that people don't take to the streets and cause unrest in the face of the economic and employment shocks brought about by AI. Until we find a way to fairly distribute the wealth generated by this productivity, owning assets that perform well in a monetary glut is one way to cope. At that time, regardless of which assets retail investors prefer, they will flock back to the market, participate in various conferences and gatherings, and that craze will definitely return. The Impact of Excessive Currency Issuance on Bitcoin Natalie Brunell: Do you think governments will print more money in the future to cope with new economic challenges than during the pandemic? Arthur Hayes: It's very likely, because it's an exponential growth process. Governments will not only need to print money to solve immediate problems, but also to print money to pay off previous debts. If you look at the growth trend of the total US national debt, you'll see it's a classic exponential growth curve. From this perspective, this is just the beginning. Imagine if AI were as powerful as people say—though I personally don't think it would be entirely so—everyone would lose their jobs, right? While this might not happen, how terrible would it be if one day everyone were replaced by robots or automated systems? The banking system might completely collapse, and then governments would have to print even more money to deal with everything. Of course, I don't think this extreme scenario will actually happen, but it can serve as a model for thinking about what the consequences might be if AI were really as powerful as people say. Career Development in the AI ​​Era: Opportunities and Challenges Natalie Brunell: If you had just graduated from college and wanted to accumulate as much wealth as possible, what would you choose to do? Arthur Hayes: Go podcast. While computers can do many things, their input still needs to come from human conversation and text. AI needs new experiences, new human experiences, to make predictive analyses and serve us. Therefore, those who can drive conversations and create fresh and unique content will become the most valuable talents in this post-AI society. Give it a try, no matter what you're good at. Whether it's writing, singing, dancing, or any other form of creative content, these are needs that AI cannot meet, but rather sources from which it needs to learn to do its job. Natalie Brunell: Many people are currently worried that creative jobs are being replaced by AI. I've seen some generated videos that look incredibly realistic, but are actually created by AI. Those videos that look like real people might just be virtual avatars; sometimes it's really hard to tell the difference. Don't you think it will become increasingly difficult to distinguish between reality and virtuality in the future? Arthur Hayes: It definitely will be difficult, which is why people are buying things like Worldcoin or developing sovereign identity verification technologies, like "human authentication." These technologies might become some kind of standard, but I'm not sure exactly what it will be; it's actually a problem that people have been researching for many years. If you think about it, when you type a prompt, like "Generate something that looks like this," the prompt is usually based on a real person, a band, or something that already exists. The AI ​​will try to imitate it. So, you should strive to be someone that AI wants to imitate in every way. Natalie Brunell: What are your favorite AI tools? Do you use them frequently in your work? Arthur Hayes: I recently started using Perplexity's tools, and I find them incredibly powerful; they're like an intelligent task orchestration platform. Some of my friends bought Mac Minis and installed Clawbot or other tools to delve into the specifics of AI implementation, but I don't have that much time or interest in studying those technical details. Perplexity's tools can process various tasks synchronously and in parallel, and can also utilize AI agents to complete tasks. I think there will be a future trend where people like me won't need to manually coordinate multiple AI agents, but can directly pay companies like Perplexity to provide tools that streamline the process. I mainly use it for research and generating graphs, and it's really convenient. Natalie Brunell: Have you seen the recent article by a researcher at Anthropics who quit his job and stated that human society is heading towards destruction? He even decided to move to London. Are you worried about the negative impacts of AI, or the dark future predicted by those pessimists? Arthur Hayes: Perhaps, but I think life is too short to worry about these things all the time. I don't know if the future will be good or bad. I've read a lot of science fiction, and there are good futures and bad futures in them. Whatever happens, it's inevitable. So, I don't worry about it too much. Maelstrom Fund: How does it affect the crypto market? Natalie Brunell: I'd like to know about your work. You are the Chief Investment Officer of Maelstrom Fund. What do you mainly do? What is the specific business of this company? Arthur Hayes: My partner Akshat and I founded Maelstrom. He previously worked for me at BitMEX, and then we founded this company in 2013. To be honest, I'm not very good at early-stage investing because I lose patience easily. We don't have limited partners (LPs), so we don't have to worry about what others think. We focus on finding profitable opportunities and on technically sound trades, getting into quality projects early on. Initially, we mainly wrote small checks, like $50,000 to $250,000, to invest in early-stage projects. We got off to a great start, at the perfect time to invest in some high-performing "junk coins." For example, Etherfi, which started as an Ethereum staking service provider and later evolved into a new type of bank, is now our advisor. We're also advisors to Pendle and Athena. Athena was a protocol idea I came up with, later implemented by Guy Young and his team, and is now the third-largest stablecoin protocol. In the early stages of the fund, we performed exceptionally well. Later, we gradually shifted towards providing advisory services. That's why you'll often see me speaking on various podcasts or at cryptocurrency conferences, sharing our latest developments and the token projects we're optimistic about. Of course, our advisory fees are typically paid in tokens, as we believe these tokens have significant appreciation potential in the future. In addition, we also venture into the liquidity trading space. I hired a colleague who previously worked with me at BitMEX and Deutsche Bank, and now we primarily focus on directional and volatility trading in cryptocurrencies. Our strategy is predominantly long, with very little shorting. Recently, we've also expanded into private equity investments. We've identified many excellent companies in the crypto industry, such as those specializing in data availability, API connectivity, and other services. They support trading, typically attracting substantial capital inflows while maintaining high profit margins. However, because these companies are often founded by non-US founders, their valuations are often undervalued. Many founders have been deeply involved in the industry for years and may want to cash out some of their shares. This is where we step in, providing substantial funding to acquire these companies, replace management, bring in new operating teams, improve the price-to-earnings ratio (P/E ratio), and then either take the company public or sell it to financial institutions looking to acquire quality assets. We are currently raising funds for this fund.

Regulation, Privacy, and the Potential Threats of Quantum Computing Technology

Natalie Brunell: Looking at recent years, we've seen the cryptocurrency industry receive increasing attention from regulators, especially regarding anti-money laundering (AML) and know-your-customer (KYC) regulations, and even crackdowns on privacy tools. Do you think this tension will continue? Especially given Bitcoin's role as a peer-to-peer exchange medium and protocol, how will the conflict evolve between this and concerns about increasing institutionalization and corporate control over Bitcoin?

Arthur Hayes:

There's no doubt that Bitcoin has become synonymous with this antagonism, and many worry that companies might introduce absurd KYC and AML mechanisms, thereby stripping Bitcoin of its privacy. These concerns are valid, but the actual implementation may be quite different.

I believe the real threat will come from AI tools that can deanonymize your transactions—these are the true "game changers." This change might not require complex new regulations; simply inputting an address and a specific person into a Large Language Model (LLM) will yield a high-probability match. In fact, this technology already exists, although the current accuracy may not be high enough, but it's happening. This is why I'm very bullish on Zcash. Zcash is essentially a privacy-focused version of Bitcoin, based on Bitcoin's code but incorporating zero-knowledge proofs. If you truly need a completely anonymous electronic cash system, Bitcoin might not be the best choice. I don't think Bitcoin's core developers will add such functionality to the protocol, while Zcash was designed to address this issue. If you're concerned about the future integration of AI technology with data from large tech companies, coupled with government control over data, then privacy coins like Zcash or Monero might be more suitable. Natalie Brunell: What are your thoughts on the potential threats posed by quantum computing technology? Arthur Hayes: I'm not too worried about quantum computing posing a threat to Bitcoin. I've discussed this issue several times with Johnny Beer from the BitMEX research team. In fact, Bitcoin Improvement Proposals (BIPs) have already proposed methods to keep Bitcoin secure in the post-quantum computing era. If you're currently concerned about quantum computing, there are some readily available solutions, such as using the latest Bitcoin address types. If you send Bitcoin to these addresses and only use them once, your Bitcoin will not be threatened by quantum computing. However, if you're still using older Bitcoin addresses from 2009, these addresses may face certain security risks in the future when quantum computing technology matures. This is because quantum computing could potentially deduce private keys from public keys and transaction records. Overall, I don't think this is an urgent issue. The community is actively researching it and has proposed some solutions that could allow Bitcoin to adapt to the post-quantum computing era. Generally speaking, I think concerns about quantum computing are more of a FUD (Fear, Uncertainty, and Doubt). Every few years, someone uses the "quantum computing panic" to hype things up, saying things like, "I won't buy Bitcoin anymore because quantum computing will crack it in the future." But in reality, the relevant solutions already exist; they just need time to be implemented. So, I'm not particularly worried about this. Will institutions push for a Bitcoin fork? Natalie Brunell: Some people predict that Bitcoin might experience a similar event to the block size debate of the past, but this time the driving force might come from the institutional level. For example, something like the "BlackRock chain" might emerge, triggering a fork and forming another chain. Do you think this will happen? If it does, what do you think the situation would be like? Arthur Hayes: If BlackRock possessed 30% of the computing power, this might be possible. But the reality is, BlackRock is no different from the rest of us; they are simply Bitcoin holders because their primary business is asset management—their role is to accept clients' assets, custody them, and collect management fees. However, those who truly influence the Bitcoin network are those involved in mining, running nodes, and other direct participants in the crypto ecosystem, and BlackRock does not engage in these activities. Therefore, the so-called "BlackRock Chain" is not a practical possibility unless they decide to purchase ASIC miners and start mining Bitcoin. If you can tell me such a future scenario, then we can discuss things like "BlackRock pushing for a Bitcoin hard fork." Otherwise, such claims are completely meaningless. Has the price of Bitcoin bottomed out? Natalie Brunell: One last question: Do you think the price of Bitcoin has bottomed out? Arthur Hayes: I'm not sure either. I recently wrote an article discussing the war situation between the US and Iran, and I think if this conflict continues, the stock market could experience a significant sell-off, and the price of Bitcoin could fall further, possibly even below $60,000. This could trigger a series of chain reactions. So, if I only had $1 to invest right now, I would choose to hold off for the time being rather than buy Bitcoin now. My view is that the longer this conflict lasts, the more likely the Federal Reserve is to start printing money to support the US war machine. And when the central bank starts printing money, that's when I'll choose to buy Bitcoin. In fact, we've already heard some signs, such as Trump might send ground troops, or that the war could last a long time, however long it may take. These statements imply that the longer the war drags on, the more likely the Federal Reserve is to resort to money printing to support the situation. Looking back on my life, this has almost always been the case with every major war in the Middle East. Therefore, if you hear someone say "war is good for Bitcoin," what they really mean is "war means printing money, and printing money is good for Bitcoin." So my advice is to wait for the money printing to happen and not try to predict the market, because you might be wrong. The news reports we see are often propaganda from mainstream media; they convey the information that authoritative institutions want us to hear, not necessarily what will actually happen. Therefore, instead of believing what they say, observe what they actually do.

data.title: Acquiring Feixiaohao at 16 times its own market value? I've investigated the suspicious points of this transaction.
data.description:
Author: Eric, Foresight News

On the evening of March 13th, Beijing time, Nasdaq-listed UTime (联代科技) announced that it plans to acquire 100% of Feixiaohao's shares for $80 million, with payment consisting of $64 million in UTime common stock or convertible bonds and $16 million in cash.

UTime stated that upon completion of the acquisition, the company will obtain all of Feixiaohao's technology platform, source code, database, and trademark rights. UTime plans to combine Feixiaohao's data capabilities with its hardware expertise to explore innovative applications that directly integrate blockchain data services into mobile devices and smart hardware, thereby entering the Web3 and blockchain data infrastructure fields.

Founded in August 2017, Feixiaohao's acquisition after nearly 10 years is not necessarily bad news.

As a leading domestic market information platform in the early stages of blockchain development, on par with Mytoken, Feixiaohao is indeed a cherished memory for many veterans in the cryptocurrency community, and many are likely pleased with this outcome. However, after delving into this nearly $100 million acquisition, I discovered numerous suspicious points behind the transaction. Suspicious Point 1: Why acquire Feixiaohao? The acquiring company, UTime, is officially named LianDai Technology in Chinese. Its address is located in the Shenzhen Software Industry Base, next to Tencent's Binhai Building. LianDai Technology was founded in 2008 and listed on Nasdaq in April 2021. Its core business in the past was simply manufacturing mobile phones, with two self-owned brands, "UTime" and "Do," and it also provided OEM and ODM services for brands such as TCL and Haier. The official website shows that its mobile terminal products have achieved cumulative global sales of over 25 million units. However, since 2024, LianDai Technology has begun to transform from traditional mobile phone manufacturing to the healthcare field, venturing into medical wearable devices. On December 31st of last year, the company announced that its Hong Kong subsidiary, UTime Technology (HK) Company Limited, had officially signed a nearly $10 million smart health device procurement agreement with Denver-based Tumu Vertex LLC, including blood pressure watches and smart rings. What's puzzling is that, despite its seemingly successful transformation into the healthcare field, it suddenly wants to enter the Web3 and blockchain data infrastructure sectors. If this "acting on a whim" approach is still somewhat understandable, then choosing Feixiaohao is simply incomprehensible. If you open the Feixiaohao app and website, you'll find that Feixiaohao actually scrapes almost no "on-chain data." It merely aggregates data and information from platforms such as exchanges and media through its API. Even this simple information aggregation is riddled with errors. At the time of writing, Ethereum's gas fee is 0.6 GWei, but Feixiaohao's data is 9 GWei. Even setting that aside, I see absolutely no need to "integrate this data into smart hardware." Using the app directly to view the data seems like a much more straightforward option. If acquiring an application like Feixiaohao cost $80 million, how much more are you prepared to spend on the more complex process of scraping on-chain data? Point of Doubt 2: Is Feixiaohao worth $80 million? If you had asked this question before 2021, I could confidently give a positive answer, but now, platforms like Coinglass, CoinAnk, and SosoValue have significantly better data scraping capabilities and information richness than Feixiaohao. SosoValue's Series A valuation was $200 million, making Feixiaohao's $80 million valuation seem high in comparison. If this comparison is still somewhat far-fetched, we can take Coingecko, which is more similar, as an example. On January 13th of this year, CoinDesk reported that Coingecko planned to be sold at a valuation of $500 million. According to SimilarWeb data, Coingecko's monthly website visits have been around 20 million in recent months, ranking among the top 5000 globally, while Feixiaohao ranks outside the top 5000. The difference in visits between the two could be tens of times. On the App Store, the Coingecko app has 26,000 reviews, while Feixiaohao has fewer than 2,000. Of course, Feixiaohao currently cannot be downloaded using domestic Apple IDs, so this data may overlook some early domestic users. However, regardless, Feixiaohao's traffic and Coingecko's traffic cannot be on the same level, not to mention the difference in brand influence. Before 2021, Feixiaohao's domestic user base, influence, and advertising effectiveness were undeniable. However, to still give it an $80 million valuation in 2026 seems somewhat overstated to me. But the main reason for this overestimation isn't based on these data comparisons, which I'll discuss later. Question 3: Can the company's financial situation support $80 million? When I wrote this, the US stock market was still open, but LianDai Technology's market capitalization was only hovering around $5 million.

Of the $80 million acquisition of Feixiaohao, $64 million will be paid in common stock or convertible bonds. This means that LianDai Technology needs to issue more than 16 times its own market capitalization just to acquire another company.

If the acquisition is completed, the shareholding ratio of all original shareholders will decrease to 6%, while Feixiaohao shareholders will hold more than 90% of LianDai Technology's shares. In other words, LianDai Technology is prepared to pay $16 million in cash and then transfer control of this listed company to Feixiaohao.

Regarding the $16 million in cash, there are some details, but before discussing the source of this money, we need to understand the company's financial situation. According to LianDai Technology's financial report submitted in August 2025, covering the period from March 2024 to March 2025, the company's cash and cash equivalents as of March 31, 2025, were $15.05 million, total assets were $28.392 million, current liabilities were $19.086 million, and total liabilities were $45.991 million. Meanwhile, the net loss for the same period was close to $100 million. It seems that at least a year ago, Linkage Technology wouldn't have been able to come up with $16 million in cash. "Coincidentally," on October 16, 2025, Linkage Technology signed a definitive securities purchase agreement with five institutional investors to sell a total of 22,727,275 "one Class A ordinary share + Class A warrants" through a registered direct offering, generating approximately $25 million in proceeds. Now it appears that this round of financing, for which no specific reason was given at the time, may have been a preparation for today. Besides these, I also found something very interesting: according to the financial report released last August, no single shareholder of LianDai Technology held more than 5% of the shares, and the shareholding ratios of company executives and many institutions were all below 1%. So, who was driving and supporting this major decision to acquire another company at a price exceeding 16 times its own market value? The Real and Fake Feixiaohao But that's not the whole story. LianDai Technology, which spent $16 million and then handed over the company, did not explicitly state which Feixiaohao they bought. In August 2024, Crypto Intelligence Orange disclosed that many key members of the Feixiaohao team had been taken away by the Inner Mongolia police for investigation. Six months had passed, and the reason was unknown. This news was later confirmed by Wu Shuo. In September, Feixiaohao officially announced that "the platform has recently been strategically acquired and integrated by a senior team," emphasizing that the new team would be responsible for operations and continue to provide services. However, in December of the same year, the original Feixiaohao team and the acquiring party began a public war of words: the original team accused the buyer of obtaining part of the source code and data after paying the initial payment, but refusing to pay the final payment, breaking the contract, and attempting to resell the source code/data, leading the original team to cancel the brand sale plan and continue operating the platform. The acquiring party countered that it had legally registered an overseas company and trademark, accused the original team of delivering incomplete source code, reselling the platform, and claimed that it invested hundreds of thousands of dollars to repair the system before it was back online. This dispute ultimately did not have a clear outcome, and two accounts appeared on X, feixiaohao.com and feixiaohao.ai, with the latter announcing the acquisition. Based on the account's description of a US-registered compliant company and international logo copyright number, I speculate that the latter is likely the buyer rumored to have acquired the company in 2024. In other words, the original Feixiaohao team is still operating, and LianDai Technology acquired the "new Feixiaohao" that previously acquired Feixiaohao. The overvaluation I mentioned in point two was based on the feixiaohao.com website and app, while .ai websites are... well, let's just say they're questionable; those interested can check them out themselves. This makes LianDai Technology's move even more perplexing. All current evidence points to them spending 80 million to buy a new team and hundreds of thousands of dollars to fix a barely deployable version, without the original team's brand assets… And this isn't even the first outrageous move by LianDai Technology this year. On February 3rd, LianDai Technology announced a strategic intent agreement with "Shenzhen Yunwei Digital" for the purchase of 500,000 intelligent servers, with a contract value estimated at approximately $50 million. LianDai Technology calls these "high-performance servers," designed to meet the growing demands of modern computing infrastructure, and stated that this agreement marks the company's official entry into the cloud infrastructure field. This "high-performance server," model "GM800," uses the RK3566 chipset and is equipped with 4GB of memory and 128GB of storage. I, like you, didn't know what the RK3566 chip was, so I looked it up: [link to image] Can anyone tell me where the "growing demand for computing infrastructure" could be met by buying 500,000 servers of this configuration at $100 each? In conclusion, the only conclusion I can draw is that the $80 million acquisition of Feixiaohao is likely just a setup.

data.title: Global financial markets are at a very fragile juncture.
data.description:
Recently, global financial markets have experienced unprecedented volatility. Driven by both geopolitical conflicts and the impending release of macroeconomic data, commodities, represented by crude oil, have experienced extreme rollercoaster rides, while the stock market has been fluctuating around key technical levels. This article will use the latest market data to analyze the underlying logic of this round of volatility, the current market structure, and the potential policy impact of the upcoming US inflation data.

Historic Oil Market Turmoil: The Fall of the $100 Mark and Options Logic

In the past 48 hours, the crude oil market has rapidly shifted from "frenzy" to "panic."

New York crude oil futures briefly broke through the $120 per barrel mark at the beginning of the week, reaching a high not seen in many years, but were immediately met with epic selling pressure. By the close of trading on March 10, New York crude oil futures had plummeted more than 11% to $87.8 per barrel, marking the largest single-day drop since March 2022; London Brent crude oil futures also fell to around $83.45 per barrel. The immediate trigger for this dramatic reversal stemmed from subtle changes in geopolitical signals. High-ranking US officials signaled that military action against Iran might "end soon" and indicated a possible reassessment of related oil sanctions, which directly alleviated extreme market concerns about the disruption of oil transport routes through the Strait of Hormuz. However, setting aside the news, the price movement itself exposed a deeper vulnerability in the market: the stampede effect in the derivatives market. In the preceding weeks of trading, as oil prices continued to climb, a large number of speculative call options were established. This concentrated one-sided position structure often triggered a mass liquidation of long positions when prices reached key resistance levels (such as the ten-year high of $120) and failed to break through. This technical sell-off, coupled with the trend-following by macro hedge funds, contributed to an extreme single-day drop of over 10%, with billions of dollars in assets being liquidated in the intense two-way game. It is worth noting that despite the sharp fluctuations in short-term spot prices, the price volatility of longer-term contracts was relatively mild; for example, the December contract price remained stable around $70 per barrel. This indicates that the current market panic is mainly focused on short-term supply expectations for near-month delivery, rather than a fundamental reversal in the long-term global supply and demand pattern. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) forecast also confirms this, believing that supply disruptions caused by geopolitical tensions will keep Brent crude oil prices above $95 for the next two months, but as the situation eases by the end of the year, the price center will gradually fall back to the $70 level. The stock market's "dead cat bounce" and the tug-of-war at technical levels directly impacted overall market sentiment, but the stock market's reaction showed a more complex structure. Major U.S. stock indices experienced a dramatic intraday reversal on Tuesday (March 10). The Dow Jones Industrial Average surged nearly 480 points at one point, but then gave back all its gains in the final minutes, ultimately closing slightly lower, indicating a severe lack of momentum at higher levels. By the close of trading that day, the S&P 500 index closed at 6781.48 points, down 0.21%; the Nasdaq index barely closed in positive territory, rising slightly by 0.01%. From a technical analysis perspective, the S&P 500's rebound high touched the 20-day moving average on the weekly chart. This level was an important support level before the recent decline, but has now become a key resistance level. Looking at the candlestick pattern, the Nasdaq formed a "doji" after hitting its previous low. This is usually interpreted as a signal that the bulls and bears have reached a temporary balance at a key level, indicating that the downward momentum has temporarily weakened and buying is beginning to enter the market. However, this signal is not enough to confirm a trend reversal. If the key price level cannot be held in subsequent trading days, this level may only be a continuation of the decline. The current market focus is on whether the index can hold above 6800 points. Looking at the volume profile, a large amount of trading volume has accumulated around 6850 points, forming an immediate resistance zone. If the bulls cannot break through the 6900-point mark with sufficient volume, thereby attracting trend traders to cover short positions, then the recent rebound is very likely just a technical "dead cat bounce," and the market will most likely retest 6600 points, or even slide towards the previous gap support. Market Structure Signals: Sector Rotation and Fund Hedging By analyzing the flow of funds between different sectors, it can be found that the market is in a typical "defensive posture." The energy sector had already recorded 11 consecutive weeks of gains before this round of oil price increases, reflecting the dual pursuit of inflation trading and safe-haven attributes by funds. Meanwhile, technology stocks, especially the highly valued software sector, continued to be under pressure during the same period. A noteworthy correlation has emerged between the technology and cryptocurrency sectors. Bitcoin and the software sector (represented by the IGV ETF) have recently shown a high price correlation. Both experienced technical rebounds after previously falling to long-term demand zones. The underlying logic is that when funds withdrew from high-risk AI concept stocks, they did not completely leave the market but instead flowed into "undervalued" sectors that had already experienced significant declines and where pessimistic sentiment had been largely cleared. At the same time, traditional safe-haven assets such as gold remained strong, firmly standing above their 20-day moving average on the daily chart. Copper prices, after withstanding the test of a "head and shoulders" pattern, held onto the key neckline, reflecting continued support from industrial demand and indicating that a trend collapse has not yet occurred. These signs collectively paint a picture of a market characterized by "overall caution and localized rotation": funds have not completely withdrawn but are instead seeking relatively safe havens in different asset classes. Macroeconomic Crossroads: Policy Game Ahead of CPI Data If oil price fluctuations amplify market sentiment, then the upcoming US February inflation data will be the cornerstone determining the macroeconomic trading logic for the coming months. The market generally expects the year-on-year increase in the February CPI (Consumer Price Index) to decline moderately, with the core CPI expected to rise by 0.3% month-on-month. However, this forecast has a significant lag – it fails to fully account for the imported inflationary pressures from the recent surge in oil prices. Due to the statistical cycle, the impact of the oil price spike will mainly be reflected in inflation data from March onwards. This means the Federal Reserve is facing an extremely complex decision-making environment. On the one hand, the job market is showing signs of cooling, with February non-farm payroll data falling short of expectations and the unemployment rate rising slightly to 4.4%. On the other hand, rising energy costs are pushing up inflation expectations again. Swap rates used to hedge inflation risk have risen significantly, indicating investors' concerns that inflation may stabilize again around 3%. The CME Group's FedWatch Tool shows that market expectations for an interest rate cut this year have cooled significantly. The probability of keeping rates unchanged in June has risen to over 57%, a stark contrast to expectations a month ago. This revision of expectations directly reflects the market's repricing of the risk of stagflation. In conclusion, the global financial market is currently at an extremely sensitive equilibrium. While the historic volatility in crude oil has temporarily subsided, it reveals that in a highly leveraged derivatives market, even the smallest geopolitical signal can be amplified many times over. The repeated struggle at key technical levels in the stock market is essentially a game between the ingrained "buy the dip" mentality and increasingly severe macroeconomic risks. The market's next direction will depend on the evolution of the following variables: whether geopolitical tensions will ease or new variables will emerge; whether upcoming inflation data will confirm or disprove the market's "stubborn inflation" expectations; and whether the Federal Reserve, facing "stagflation" pressures, will prioritize economic growth or price stability. Until then, the market's main characteristics will be high volatility and extreme structural divergence. From a technical analysis perspective, whether the S&P 500 can hold the key support level of 6600 points and regain a foothold above the 20-week moving average will be the core basis for judging the strength of the medium-term trend. Before this uncertainty subsides, maintaining sensitivity to macroeconomic data and respecting key price levels given by charts may be an effective strategy for dealing with the complex situation.

Tether's Chief Investment Officer to Step Down After Leading Large-Scale Investment Deals

On March 13, Richard Heathcote, Chief Investment Officer of Tether Holdings, will step down from his current position. Heathcote was previously responsible for managing the assets underpinning its main stablecoin reserves and led a series of investment deals spanning multiple sectors, from football clubs to humanoid robots. In response to inquiries from Bloomberg News, the company stated that Heathcote will transition to a non-executive advisory role and will no longer assume day-to-day management responsibilities. His deputy, Zachary Lyons, will succeed him.

... AI Hot Topics 1. NetEase Smart Enterprise Releases Security Skill to Provide Pre-emptive Security Protection for "Lobster" (a term referring to a specific type of AI application) On March 13th, NetEase Smart Enterprise's YiDun Security Team released a security skill (yidun-skill-sec), which combines static behavioral analysis with cloud-based threat intelligence to generate a quantitative security score. This skill can accurately identify risks such as malware, data theft, privilege abuse, prompt injection, and code obfuscation before code execution, providing pre-emptive security protection for the ClawHub ecosystem and helping AI intelligent agent applications operate more securely and stably. It is understood that a more comprehensive Agent version will be released in the middle to late part of this month.

2. National Cybersecurity Information Center Issues OpenClaw Security Risk Warning

March 13th news: Monitoring data from the National Cybersecurity Information Center shows that there are currently over 200,000 active OpenClaw internet assets globally, of which approximately 23,000 are active within China, showing explosive growth. These assets are mainly concentrated in internet-rich regions such as Beijing, Shanghai, Guangdong, Zhejiang, Sichuan, and Jiangsu. The large number of OpenClaw assets exposed to the internet poses significant security risks and makes them highly vulnerable to cyberattacks. In particular, the behavior of OpenClaw agents is uncontrollable, making management difficult. OpenClaw agents are prone to losing control of permissions during instruction execution, leading to unauthorized task execution and disregard for user instructions. This could result in the deletion of user data, the theft of user information, and takeover of user terminal devices, causing significant economic losses.

...

3. OpenClaw Free Deployment: Shanghai Telecom Provides Computing Power Support for SMEs

March 13th news: Since the beginning of this year, AI intelligent agents such as OpenClaw have sparked a craze in China. With the popularization of AI applications, the demand for computing power has increased exponentially. To meet the AI ​​computing power needs of more SMEs and startups, Shanghai Telecom has launched computing power benefits, further lowering the threshold for AI applications. From now until December 31st, Shanghai enterprise customers can be among the first to experience the OpenClaw intelligent service pre-installed on the Tianyi AI Cloud PC, achieving "out-of-the-box use" without any configuration. Before June 30th, Shanghai Telecom will also provide enterprise customers with 100 hours of free dedicated computing power experience.

...

4.a16z: AI Proxy Payment Volume Lower Than Reported Data, But Adoption Is Growing

On March 12, Noah Levine, a partner at a16z, posted on the X platform that a previous Bloomberg article cited data from x402.org claiming that AI proxies completed $24 million in payments in 30 days. However, Allium Labs data shows that the transaction volume of AI proxies during the same period was approximately $3 million, and after excluding inflated transactions, the actual figure was approximately $1.6 million. Levine stated that current AI payment activity is mainly concentrated in the developer tools field, including web crawling platform Firecrawl, AI browser Browserbase, and AI image platform Freepik. Companies such as Stripe, Cloudflare, and Vercel have integrated the x402 payment standard developed by Coinbase, and Google has also embedded the system into its proxy payment protocol. Levine stated that the transaction volume of $1.6 million is not large, but the infrastructure built around it is considerable, and these companies are betting on the market prospects of AI proxies becoming the default buyer.

In addition, Coinbase announced that its x402 Facilitator now supports Polygon, allowing developers to accept USDC payments on Polygon, Base, and Solana. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong stated that AI-assisted transaction volume will soon surpass that of humans.

DeFi Hot Topics

1. On-chain Automated Market Maker Platform Space LP to Launch Soon

According to official reports, the on-chain automated market maker and smart wealth management platform Space LP will officially launch within 48 hours after the node recruitment ends. As the only lead generation wealth management product of DBAEX Digital Asset Bank, the launch of the Space-LP automated market maker system marks the ecosystem's official transition from the node construction phase to the wealth management product operation phase. It is understood that Space LP constructs a 24-hour automated market making and automatic compound interest rollover intelligent wealth management closed loop through the interaction of four protocols: staking, minting, secondary market, and treasury.

Users participate in market making with USDT, and the smart contract automatically completes liquidity allocation and profit reinvestment, achieving a minimalist "allocation equals profit" experience. Space node recruitment will officially close at 12:00 on March 15th, and the LP automated market maker system is expected to launch before 12:00 on March 17th.

2. Vitalik: FLI cashed out approximately $500 million of its donated SHIB tokens

On March 13th, Vitalik Buterin posted on the X platform that he received a large amount of SHIB and other tokens, and at the peak of the price, he donated half to CryptoRelief and the other half to FLI. He originally thought FLI could cash out at most $10 million to $25 million, but FLI ultimately cashed out approximately $500 million.

...

March 13th news: According to data from Agentscan and 8004scan, BNB SmartChain currently has over 34,000 registered AI agents using the ERC-8004 standard, surpassing Ethereum's 14,000 on the mainnet and Base's 16,500, becoming the network with the most on-chain AI agents. Since the beginning of this year, the total number of ERC-8004 agents across all chains has increased from 337 to nearly 130,000, an increase of over 39,000%. On-chain activity has also surged. On March 10th, the daily transaction volume of agents on BSC reached a record high of nearly 523,000; on March 11th, the daily trading volume of agent-driven DEXs also exceeded $18.1 million.

This means that the current sUSDe unstaking period will be reduced from a fixed 7 days since the project's launch to just 1 day. In the future, the cooling-off period will dynamically vary between 1 and 7 days, depending on the composition of liquid assets in the USDe reserves.
